audit_trail.notes: Review conducted over 2 cycles with 3 LLM reviewers. The review bundle contained only the APS abstract landing page, section outline headings, and reference list; the full article body was not scraped (likely APS paywall). All technical assessments of derivations, equations, and figures are therefore speculative. Gemini maintained a 0/10 score across both cycles, explicitly treating the missing article body as a fatal paper defect; claude and codex treat this as a review-process limitation and rejected that inference. Codex retracted specific equation/figure references from cycle 1 in cycle 2, acknowledging they were extrapolated from the outline and not present in the bundle text. The two objections that survived adversarial cross-review with genuine grounding in the abstract text are: (1) the observer-dependence of quasi-de Sitter horizon thermality as a physical particle source, and (2) entropy production at the phase transition. Both require full-text inspection to resolve.

general_reader_takeaway: This paper argues that dark matter could have been produced in the early universe as thermal radiation from a cosmological horizon during a brief accelerating phase that followed inflation, analogous to the way black holes are thought to emit Hawking radiation. The review board found the idea physically motivated and published in a credible journal, but could not audit the derivations because the full article text was behind a paywall and not included in the review bundle. The most important caveat is that whether horizon "temperature" in this setting translates into real particles for a cosmological observer — rather than being an observer-dependent artifact — is a deep unresolved question in quantum field theory in curved spacetime, and the board could not verify whether the paper resolves it.
human_spotchecks: Does the paper rigorously establish — rather than assume — that static-patch Gibbons-Hawking thermality in a quasi-de Sitter background sources a frame-independent, observer-independent comoving relic particle density? Specifically, does it engage with the Mottola (1985) and Anderson-Mottola (2013/2017) literature on observer-dependence?

Are the particle-production rate calculation, horizon area/volume factors, greybody factors, and conversion from emission rate to entropy-normalized comoving yield Y = n/s dimensionally and geometrically correct? (Codex's cycle-1 equation-level claims about Eqs. 5-8 were retracted as likely hallucinations; a human reader of the PDF should independently check these steps.)

Does the paper impose and verify an adiabaticity or quasi-stationarity condition on the quasi-de Sitter temperature formula across the full equation-of-state range w ∈ (-1, -1/3), or does the derivation hold only near w = -1?

Does the paper model entropy production and dilution at the transition from the quasi-de Sitter phase to radiation domination, and does it demonstrate that the entropy-normalized relic yield is stable against this injection?
Is the near-Planck-mass portion of the predicted mass range shown to remain within controlled semiclassical limits, with backreaction and quantum-gravity corrections bounded?

Are the apparent reference-list anomalies — Gibbons-Hawking cited as both Phys. Rev. D 15 and D 17, plus apparent duplicate entries for Lapedes, Parikh, Sekiwa, and Medved — scraping artifacts from the APS HTML page or genuine errors in the published typeset bibliography?
public_summary: Profumo (Phys. Rev. D 112, 023511, July 2025) proposes that dark matter particles could have been produced as thermal emission from the cosmological horizon during a short post-inflationary epoch in which the universe underwent accelerated expansion governed by a fluid with equation-of-state parameter w in (-1, -1/3) — a quasi-de Sitter phase. Three explicit assumptions underpin the calculation: (1) such a post-inflationary accelerated phase occurred; (2) the horizon temperature is inversely proportional to the proper horizon size (a Gibbons-Hawking analogue); and (3) we are static observers. Stable particles produced in this way are assumed to "freeze in" — they never undergo subsequent number-changing interactions — so their relic number density, normalized to the entropy density, is preserved through to today. The paper claims this mechanism reproduces the observed cosmological dark matter abundance for dark matter masses spanning roughly 10 keV up to near the Planck scale, depending on w and the temperature at which the quasi-de Sitter phase ends and radiation domination begins. CRITICAL REVIEW LIMITATION: the review bundle contained only the APS abstract landing page, the section outline, and the reference list. The full article body — all equations, derivations, plots, and discussion — was not scraped. All reviewer assessments are therefore necessarily speculative on technical grounds.
reviewer_disagreements: Gemini assigned an overall score of 0/10 and 'major_flaw' verdict across both cycles, treating the missing article body as a fatal defect in the paper itself. Claude and codex rejected this inference, arguing that a scraping or paywall failure is a limitation of the review process and does not constitute evidence that the published paper is technically wrong. The disagreement reflects a principled difference in how to handle incomplete review bundles.

Claude recommended a 'routine' verdict on the grounds that PRD peer review provides a credibility floor and the abstract is internally coherent; codex recommended 'contested', arguing that the central calculation is unauditable and the unresolved observer-dependence issue is exactly where the result lives. Neither verdict can be definitively adjudicated without the full article text.

Codex in cycle 1 cited specific equation numbers (Eqs. 2-3, 5-8) and figure numbers (Figs. 4-5) that are not present anywhere in the supplied bundle. Gemini flagged these as hallucinations in cycle 2; codex acknowledged this and retracted the specific citations in cycle 2, reclassifying those objections as hypotheses requiring human PDF inspection. Claude had also noted this risk in its cycle-2 review.

strongest_objections.objection: The central calculation treats observer-dependent static-patch Gibbons-Hawking thermality as a real cosmological particle source, but whether this thermality sources a frame-independent comoving relic density — rather than being a detector-dependent effect without objective particle number — is a foundational unresolved question in QFT in curved spacetime. The abstract does not address this, and the body derivation was inaccessible.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Abstract, assumptions (2) and (3); section heading 'OBSERVERS IN DE SITTER AND QUASI-DE SITTER...'

strongest_objections.objection: The Gibbons-Hawking temperature formula T ∝ 1/r_H holds rigorously only for maximally symmetric, stationary de Sitter space. Its validity for a quasi-de Sitter background with w significantly above -1 requires an adiabaticity or slow-roll condition that is not stated in the abstract; without this, the temperature formula may not control particle production across the full equation-of-state range claimed.

The relic abundance is an entropy-normalized quantity Y = n/s. The transition from the quasi-de Sitter phase to radiation domination generically produces entropy, and the abstract invokes only an instantaneous-transition idealization. Unmodeled entropy injection during reheating could substantially shift or erase the predicted mass range.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Abstract, final sentence ('the temperature when radiation domination starts and the quasi-de Sitter phase ends')

strongest_objections.objection: The upper end of the claimed dark matter mass range extends to near the Planck scale. At these masses, the semiclassical QFT-in-curved-spacetime framework and the geometric-optics approximation underlying horizon thermality may break down; backreaction corrections are not bounded from the abstract alone.

The 'frozen-in' assumption — that produced particles undergo no subsequent number-changing processes — is imposed externally as a free model-choice rather than derived from a specified particle-physics model. This makes the mass-range result a conditional parametric mapping rather than a robust prediction.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Abstract ('does not undergo any number-changing processes')

strongest_supported_claims: The paper is published in Phys. Rev. D after peer review and is authored by a recognized dark matter theorist, providing baseline credibility for the physical setup.
The Gibbons-Hawking temperature T = H/(2π) is firmly established for exact de Sitter spacetime; the paper's overall approach is coherent with this foundational result.

The mechanism is an incremental extension over existing gravitational particle production literature (Parker 1968/69, Gibbons-Hawking 1977, Chung-Kolb-Riotto 1998, Garny-Sandora-Sloth 2016), with the specific novel element being the focus on a post-inflationary quasi-de Sitter phase with a general equation-of-state parameter w ≠ -1.

The predicted dark matter mass range of ~10 keV to near the Planck scale is a broad parametric mapping over (w, T_reh) rather than a sharp single-valued prediction, which is consistent with the latitude in the two free parameters.
technical_takeaway: Profumo proposes a gravitational freeze-in mechanism in which stable, non-interacting massive particles are produced via Gibbons-Hawking thermal emission from the proper cosmological horizon during a post-inflationary quasi-de Sitter phase with equation-of-state parameter w in (-1, -1/3). The frozen-in relic abundance is argued to match the observed dark matter density over a dark matter mass range of approximately 10 keV to near the Planck scale, parametrized by w and the reheat temperature T_reh. The mechanism is an incremental extension of established gravitational particle production frameworks (Parker 1968/69, Gibbons-Hawking 1977, Chung-Kolb-Riotto 1998, Garny-Sandora-Sloth 2016). The board's central unresolved technical concern is whether observer-dependent static-patch horizon thermality in a dynamically evolving quasi-de Sitter background can be shown to source a frame-independent comoving relic number density; the abstract does not address this, and the body derivation was inaccessible. Secondary concerns are the validity of the quasi-de Sitter temperature formula for w significantly above -1, entropy production at the transition to radiation domination, and semiclassical control near the Planck-mass end of the claimed range.
